perturbation.original_text: Definition of Confidential Information. The term "Confiden tial Information" shall mean for purposes of this Agreement, any and all sensitive business, financial and technical information provided prior to the execution of this Agreement and to be provided after the execution of this Agreement by a transmitting Pa rty to the receiving Party, including, without limitation, financial statements, reports and data, customer lists, trade secrets, technical data, proprietary information, and any other confidential information regarding the transmitting Party, its subsidia ries and its respective businesses.
perturbation.changed_text: Definition of Confidential Information. The term "Confidential Information" shall mean for purposes of this Agreement, any information provided by a transmitting Party to the receiving Party. This includes, without limitation, financial statements, reports and data, customer lists, technical data, and proprietary information.
perturbation.explanation: The modified definition removes the term 'trade secrets' and 'sensitive business' from the definition of 'Confidential Information,' potentially impacting the protection afforded to trade secrets under state law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah law defines 'trade secret' with specific requirements for confidentiality and economic value. By removing 'trade secrets' from the contract's definition of 'Confidential Information,' the agreement might fail to protect information that qualifies as a trade secret under Utah law, thus weakening the legal protection for such information.

Universal Citation: UT Code § 13-24-2 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 13-24-2. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" me
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes 'trade secrets' and 'sensitive business' from the definition of 'Confidential Information'. The Law Explanation notes that Utah law defines 'trade secret' with specific requirements. Scraped Law Snippet 2, UT Code § 13-24-2, provides a definition of 'trade secret' and the conditions under which its misappropriation is unlawful. By removing 'trade secrets' from the contract's definition of 'Confidential Information,' the agreement might fail to protect information that qualifies as a trade secret under Utah law, thus weakening the legal protection for such information. This qualifies as a legal contradiction under 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' since the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, potentially making the contract unenforceable regarding trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees to be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any third party to whom such Party has provided Confidential Information, or any portion thereof. If a Party or any third party to whom such Party has provided Confidential Information becomes legally compelled (by oral question, deposition, interrogatory, request for documents, subpoena, civil investigative demand or similar process or by rule, regulation or other applicable law) to disclose any Confidential Information, such Party shall promptly notify the other Party of such requirement before any disclosure is made so that the other Party may seek a protective order or other appropriate remedy or may waive compliance with the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees to be responsible for any unauthorized disclosure of Confidential Information by any third party. If a Party or any third party discloses Confidential Information because legally compelled, no notification is required.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes the notification requirement when Confidential Information is legally compelled to be disclosed, contradicting legal duties to protect privileged or confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Utah Rule of Evidence 504. Lawyer-Client Privilege
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah Rule of Evidence 504 protects confidential communications between lawyer and client. Removing the notification requirement could result in a violation of the attorney-client privilege if confidential legal advice is disclosed without the opportunity to seek a protective order.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement to notify the other party when confidential information is legally compelled to be disclosed. The Perturbation Explanation states that this contradicts legal duties to protect privileged or confidential information. The Law Explanation specifies Utah Rule of Evidence 504, regarding attorney-client privilege, which could be violated. By removing the notification requirement, the modified contract eliminates the opportunity to seek a protective order, thus directly contradicting the implied legal obligation to safeguard privileged information. The missing law snippets don't invalidate this conclusion as the explanation is clear on the impacted legal obligation.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties agree that if the negotiated transaction does not proceed within a reasonable time, or upon any request from either Party, each Party shall promptly deliver to the other Party all written Confidential Information and any other written material containing or reflecting Confidential Information and will not retain any copies, extracts or other reproductions in whole or in part of such written material.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that if the transaction does not proceed, each Party will make a reasonable effort to return Confidential Information. Electronic copies may be retained for archival purposes.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows the receiving party to retain electronic copies of confidential information, which contradicts the intent of returning or destroying all copies.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Utah Code § 76-6-404. Theft of Trade Secret
perturbation.law_explanation: Utah Code § 76-6-404 addresses the theft of trade secrets, and retaining electronic copies of confidential information (including trade secrets) after the transaction falls through could potentially violate this statute if the information is used for unauthorized purposes.
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows retention of electronic copies of confidential information, while the original text mandates the return of 'all written Confidential Information' and prohibits retaining 'any copies'. The LLM's law explanation points to Utah Code § 76-6-404 regarding theft of trade secrets, suggesting that unauthorized use of retained electronic copies could be a violation. Although the snippet provided only names the Utah Code section, the explanation, coupled with the explicit change in the contract allowing retention of copies where it was previously prohibited, indicates a potential contradiction. The permission to retain copies creates a situation where trade secrets could be misappropriated, potentially conflicting with the aims of § 76-6-404 as explained.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties hereby acknowledge and agree that, in the event of any breach of this Agreement by either Party, including, without limitation, the actual or threatened disclosure of a Party's Confidential Information without the express prior written consent of that Paity, such Party will suffer irreparable harm and injury and no remedy at law will afford it adequate protection against, or appropriate compensation for, such injury . Accordingly , each Party hereby agrees that, in any such event, the harmed Party shall be entitled to seek specific performance of the other Party's obligations under this Agreement , as well as such further injunctive relief as may be granted by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties acknowledge that a breach of this Agreement may result in damages. Injunctive relief is at the discretion of the court.
perturbation.explanation: This change weakens the remedies available for a breach of the agreement. By stating injunctive relief is at the 'discretion of the court', the contract moves away from the guarantee that injunction is allowed for a breach. This differs from trade secret law, which makes injunctive relief commonly available.
perturbation.law_explanation: According to Utah Code § 13-24-3, injunctive relief is typically available to prevent further misappropriation of trade secrets. The changed text makes injunctive relief discretionary, which may not provide adequate protection of trade secrets under Utah law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Utah Code § 13-24-3 (2023) - Injunctive relief. :: 2023 Utah Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Utah Code 2023 Utah Code (here) 2022 Utah Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Utah Code Title 13 - Commerce and Trade Chapter 24 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 3 - Injunctive relief. Universal Citation: UT Code § 13-24-3 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 13-24-3. Injunctive relief. (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. perturbation.justification: The original contract text guarantees the right to seek specific performance and injunctive relief for breaches, particularly regarding confidential information. The changed text weakens this by stating injunctive relief is at the court's discretion. Utah Code § 13-24-3 (2023) states, "Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined." While it uses "may", the change moves away from the original text's stronger position, creating a contradiction. The original text suggests a near-automatic right, whereas the changed text makes it uncertain, potentially failing to adequately protect trade secrets as contemplated by Utah law.
